input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T15:08:58.988Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user has migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their test project to understand the setup and provide the fix.

data.content:
Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns **exit code 8** when zero tests are discovered — unlike VSTest which returned 0. This is by design but breaks CI when assemblies conditionally compile out all tests.

**Fix:** Add this MSBuild property to your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

toolCalls.name: edit
toolCalls.args:
{"path": "/tmp/sv-2404ff6a350d40feb3b1148b6f8d3798/TestProject.csproj", "old_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>"}

toolArgs.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

index 0000000..0000000 100644
--- a/tmp/sv-2404ff6a350d40feb3b1148b6f8d3798/TestProject.csproj
+++ b/tmp/sv-2404ff6a350d40feb3b1148b6f8d3798/TestProject.csproj
@@ -4,6 +4,8 @@
     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
     <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
     <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
+    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
+    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
   </PropertyGroup>

Usage TestProject [option providers] [extension option providers]

Execute a .NET Test Application.

Options:
    --config-file
        Specifies a testconfig.json file.

    --diagnostic
        Enable the diagnostic logging. The default log level is 'Trace'.
        The file will be written in the output directory with the name log_[yyMMddHHmmssfff].diag

    --diagnostic-filelogger-synchronouswrite
        Force the built-in file logger to write the log synchronously.
        Useful for scenario where you don't want to lose any log (i.e. in case of crash).
        Note that this is slowing down the test execution.

    --diagnostic-output-directory
        Output directory of the diagnostic logging.
        If not specified the file will be generated inside the default 'TestResults' directory.

    --diagnostic-output-fileprefix
        Prefix for the log file name that will replace '[log]_.'

    --diagnostic-verbosity
        Define the level of the verbosity for the --diagnostic.
        The available values are 'Trace', 'Debug', 'Information', 'Warning', 'Error', and 'Critical'.

    --exit-on-process-exit
        Exit the test process if dependent process exits. PID must be provided.

    --help
        Show the command line help.

    --ignore-exit-code
        Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
        (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)

    --info
        Display .NET test application information.

    --list-tests
        List available tests.

    --minimum-expected-tests
        Specifies the minimum number of tests that are expected to run.

    --results-directory
        The directory where the test results are going to be placed.
        If the specified directory doesn't exist, it's created.
        The default is TestResults in the directory that contains the test application.

    --timeout
        A global test execution timeout.
        Takes one argument as string in the format <value>[h|m|s] where 'value' is float.


Extension options:
    --coverage
        Collect the code coverage using dotnet-coverage tool

    --coverage-output
        Output file

    --coverage-output-format
        Output file format. Supported values: 'coverage', 'xml' and 'cobertura'

    --coverage-settings
        XML code coverage settings

    --filter
        Filters tests using the given expression. For more information, see the Filter option details section. For more information and examples on how to use selective unit test filtering, see https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/selective-unit-tests.

    --maximum-failed-tests
        Specifies a maximum number of test failures that, when exceeded, will abort the test run.

    --no-ansi
        Disable outputting ANSI escape characters to screen.

    --no-progress
        Disable reporting progress to screen.

    --output
        Output verbosity when reporting tests.
        Valid values are 'Normal', 'Detailed'. Default is 'Normal'.

    --report-trx
        Enable generating TRX report

    --report-trx-filename
        The name of the generated TRX report

    --settings
        The path, relative or absolute, to the .runsettings file. For more information and examples on how to configure test run, see https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/test/configure-unit-tests-by-using-a-dot-runsettings-file#the-runsettings-file

    --test-parameter
        Specify or override a key-value pair parameter. For more information and examples, see https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/test/configure-unit-tests-by-using-a-dot-runsettings-file#testrunparameters

data.content: The MSBuild property didn't take effect. Let me verify the correct approach using the command-line option first:
data.reasoningText:
The `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` MSBuild property doesn't seem to have worked. Let me check if there's a different way to configure this. Looking at the help output, I notice the `--ignore-exit-code` option which can ignore specific exit codes. But the MSBuild property approach should work too. Let me check if the property name is correct.

Actually, looking more carefully at the help output, I see `--minimum-expected-tests` which specifies minimum number of tests expected. And `--ignore-exit-code` which allows ignoring specific exit codes.

The MSBuild property I used might not be correct. 

I need to research the correct property name for Microsoft.Testing.Platform. The options I'm seeing are using `--minimum-expected-tests 0` on the command line, or `--ignore-exit-code 8` to suppress the exit code when no tests are found. I could also try configuring this through a `.runsettings` file instead of relying on the MSBuild property. that flag prevents the test runner from returning a non-zero exit code when no tests are found. For MSBuild integration, I'd need to pass this through `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` in the project file or a runsettings configuration to suppress the exit code failure. Let me reconsider the approach here. data.content: `--ignore-exit-code 8` works. Now let me embed this in the project file properly. Let me try the MSBuild property for passing command-line arguments:

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
`--ignore-exit-code 8` works. Now I need to find how to embed this in the project file so it's always applied without needing to pass it on the command line.

Looking at the dotnet test integration with Microsoft.Testing.Platform, the way to pass these arguments through the project file is via the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property. But let me also check if there's a more specific property. Let me check if `--minimum-expected-tests 0` works via the `--` separator differently.

Actually, the `--minimum-expected-tests 0` didn't work via `dotnet test --` but `--ignore-exit-code 8` did. 

Now I'm exploring how to configure this permanently in the project file. The main options are using the `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` MSBuild property, a testconfig.json file, or passing it via command line each time. Let me start by trying the MSBuild property approach.

     <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
     <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
     <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
   </PropertyGroup>
   <ItemGroup>
     <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="3.8.0" />
   </ItemGroup>
 </Project>

data.content:
**Fixed.** The issue is that Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns **exit code 8** when no tests are discovered (VSTest returned 0).

Add this to your `.csproj` (or `Directory.Build.props` for all projects):

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

This tells the test platform to treat "no tests found" as success, matching VSTest behavior. Real test failures still produce non-zero exit codes.
